_meta.purpose: STAGING period-character candidates for P8, feeding the P4 character registry's future expansion (recurring named NPCs / era figures). NOT live data. Owner reviews before merge.
_meta.field_notes: Each candidate: name, role, era{from,to}, archetypeFamily (maps to data/archetypes/archetypes.json family id — patrician|gunslinger|quant|dealmaker|operator|rainmaker|prodigy|lifer — for voice/portrait/stat reuse), traits[], signature_style (one-liner bark voice), portraitDescriptor (Two Point <-> Persona caricature prompt seed per STAR_02 §9 / archetypes.json portraitDescriptor convention), source provenance. Real historical figures — portraits to be generated later (P9). 'cautionary_ghost' flags figures intended as off-screen reputational hazards, not hireable NPCs.

candidates.name: William McChesney Martin
candidates.signature_style: Removes the punch bowl just as the party gets going — and dares the President to stop him.
candidates.portraitDescriptor: lean patrician central banker in his fifties, silver-templed, sober dark suit and watch-chain, the calm half-smile of a man who has already decided; Persona-caricature bust, muted Federal-Reserve marble palette

source.source_chunk_or_page: chunk 007 pp.64-65 (Martin, punch-bowl / leaning-against-the-wind); Meltzer 1951 doc p.48, pp.454-458 (Dec 1965 hike over LBJ; 'independence within the government')

candidates.name: Marriner Eccles
candidates.role: Former Chairman, Federal Reserve; New Deal architect
candidates.signature_style: The banker who rewrote his own institution's rules from inside the Roosevelt White House.
candidates.portraitDescriptor: weathered Utah banker, square jaw, rimless spectacles, plain 1940s double-breasted suit, the stubborn look of a self-made reformer; Persona-caricature bust, sepia New-Deal palette

source.source_chunk_or_page: chunk 007 p.65; Meltzer 1951 doc p.50 (resigned July 1951 after ~17 years)

candidates.name: Allan Sproul
candidates.role: President, Federal Reserve Bank of New York (to 1956)
candidates.signature_style: The New York man who thought the Desk should steer the whole curve, not just the bills.
candidates.portraitDescriptor: distinguished mid-century New York banker, horn-rimmed glasses, conservative gray suit, an air of quiet authority over the trading desk; Persona-caricature bust, Wall-Street-grey palette

source.source_chunk_or_page: chunk 007 pp.50-62 (NY Fed president, bills-only rivalry, resigned 1956)

candidates.name: Arthur Burns
candidates.signature_style: Lectures you on central-bank duty, then prints the money his old friend in the White House wants.
candidates.portraitDescriptor: stout academic central banker, center-parted gray hair, pipe, rumpled professorial suit, half-moon glasses; the cautionary elder; Persona-caricature bust, dim wood-panel palette

source.source_chunk_or_page: chunk 008 pp.67-68 (1972 pump-priming, 'embarrassing odor'); Meltzer 1951 doc pp.605-607 (Penn Central 'prime mover')

candidates.name: John Connally
candidates.signature_style: Broke the gold standard with a smile and dared the world to complain.
candidates.portraitDescriptor: tall imposing Texan in an immaculate dark suit, slicked silver hair, broad confident grin; the dealmaker-politician; Persona-caricature bust, executive-blue palette

source.source_chunk_or_page: Ch.6 pp.753-775 (drove the NEP one-shot announcement); Greider chunk 008 p.68

candidates.name: Paul Volcker
candidates.role: U.S. Treasury Undersecretary for Monetary Affairs (from 1969)
candidates.signature_style: The rumpled technocrat who could bury a hostile senator in monetary esoterica.
candidates.portraitDescriptor: very tall, stooped, cheap cigar, ill-fitting suit, balding with a fringe, unreadable expression; the frugal genius; Persona-caricature bust, austere grey-brown palette

source.source_chunk_or_page: chunk 008 pp.68-69 (Treasury career, frugality, foresaw end of Bretton Woods); Meltzer 1970 doc Ch.5-6

candidates.name: Robert Roosa
candidates.signature_style: Rigged a web of currency swaps to hold the dollar's peg one more year — and one more after that.
candidates.portraitDescriptor: precise, bookish Treasury official, neat dark suit, reading glasses, ledger in hand; the system-defender; Persona-caricature bust, ledger-green palette

source.source_chunk_or_page: Ch.5 p.684 (chose capital controls over floating; thought flexible rates unworkable)

candidates.name: A. P. Giannini
candidates.signature_style: Built the world's biggest bank by lending to immigrants and farmers nobody else would touch.
candidates.portraitDescriptor: broad-shouldered Italian-American banker, heavy brows, warm commanding presence, plain dark suit; the people's banker, late in life; Persona-caricature bust, golden-California palette

source.source_chunk_or_page: well-known banking history; NOT found in KB extractions (Levy postwar chapters missing). Flag needs_web_research for color detail.

candidates.name: George S. Moore
candidates.role: President / Chairman, First National City Bank (Citibank)
candidates.signature_style: Wanted his bank everywhere money moved — and pushed it overseas before the rules caught up.
candidates.portraitDescriptor: energetic mid-century money-center banker, confident jaw, tailored suit, mid-handshake; the empire-builder; Persona-caricature bust, Citibank-blue palette

source.source_chunk_or_page: well-known Citibank history; NOT named in KB extractions. Flag needs_web_research.

candidates.name: Walter Wriston
candidates.role: Rising executive then President, First National City Bank (Citibank)
traits: believed banks should grow without limit
candidates.signature_style: Invented his way around every ceiling Washington built — and dared it to keep up.
candidates.portraitDescriptor: sharp, intense banker in his fifties, lean face, dark-rimmed glasses, immaculate suit, the look of a man certain he is right; the regulation-slayer; Persona-caricature bust, cold-steel-blue palette

source.source_chunk_or_page: Eurodollar '51st state' context chunk 007 p.58 (Wriston/Citibank not named directly — attribution from general knowledge). Flag needs_web_research for specifics.

candidates.name: David Rockefeller
candidates.role: Senior executive then President, Chase Manhattan Bank
candidates.signature_style: Banks heads of state on a first-name basis and treats foreign policy as part of the loan book.
candidates.portraitDescriptor: polished establishment banker, neat side-part, half-rimmed glasses, bespoke suit, diplomat's poise; the white-shoe statesman; Persona-caricature bust, Chase-navy palette

source.source_chunk_or_page: well-known; Greider names 19th-c. John D. Rockefeller only. Flag needs_web_research.

candidates.name: Charles E. Mitchell (archetype ghost)
candidates.role: Cautionary memory — the 1920s 'Sunshine Charley' of National City Bank
candidates.signature_style: The smiling salesman whose name old bankers still invoke to mean 'don't become him.'
candidates.portraitDescriptor: ghostly sepia portrait of a 1920s banker, slick hair, too-bright salesman's smile, faded gilt frame; invoked as a warning, never present; Persona-caricature bust, faded-photograph palette

source.source_chunk_or_page: well-known pre-1945 cautionary figure used as a period 'ghost' per prompt; NOT in KB extractions.
